does: This paper introduces BadGPT, the first backdoor attack targeting reinforcement learning-based fine-tuning of language models like InstructGPT. The attack works by injecting a backdoor into the reward model that guides the fine-tuning process, allowing attackers to manipulate the language model's behavior during training. The authors demonstrate the attack's feasibility through experiments on IMDB movie reviews, showing that an attacker can trigger specific unwanted outputs in the compromised model when certain backdoor triggers are present in the input.
connects: This work builds on the established literature on backdoor attacks in deep learning, extending threat models previously explored in image classification and NLP tasks to the emerging paradigm of RL-based alignment. It responds to growing concerns about the security of instruction-tuned models like InstructGPT and ChatGPT, which use reward models trained on human feedback to align language models with user preferences. The paper connects to earlier work on adversarial attacks against language models and poisoning attacks on training data, but uniquely targets the reward model as a vulnerability point in the RL fine-tuning pipeline that previous security research had not thoroughly examined.
enables: This research opens up new directions in understanding the security vulnerabilities of RLHF-aligned language models and reward models specifically. It points toward future work on defending reward models against backdoor injection, including robustness certification and detection mechanisms. The findings motivate the need for secure reward model training procedures, verification of training data integrity, and evaluation methodologies that can detect whether a language model has been compromised through its reward signal. More broadly, it highlights the importance of security considerations in the design of AI alignment techniques as they become deployed at scale.
